title: Jeremiah 30
subtitle: The Book of Consolation Begins

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Covenant Prophecy

sections.header: Verses 1-11 -- "I Will Restore the Fortunes of My People"

heb.paragraph: God promises to "restore the fortunes" (hashib et-shebut) of Israel and Judah (v.3). The phrase is ambiguous: shebut can mean "captivity" (a return from exile) or "fortune" (a restoration of prosperity). Both meanings operate simultaneously -- the return from physical exile coincides with spiritual renewal.

refs.note: "Comfort, comfort my people" -- Isaiah's parallel opening to the consolation oracles. Both prophets shift from judgment to hope at their rhetorical climax.
"In all things God works for the good" -- the NT echo of Jeremiah's assurance that suffering has a redemptive purpose.
"A time of distress such as has not happened" -- Daniel's "time of trouble" draws on Jeremiah's language.

notes.note: "Write in a book all the words I have spoken to you." The command to write emphasizes permanence -- these promises are to be preserved, studied, and claimed across generations.
Restoration of "Israel and Judah" -- both kingdoms included. The promise transcends the divided monarchy and envisions a reunited people.
"How awful that day will be! It will be a time of trouble for Jacob, but he will be saved out of it." The trouble is acknowledged but bounded: it ends in salvation.
"They will serve the LORD their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them." The Davidic promise from 23:5-6 returns -- the messianic king is part of the restoration vision.

Calvin reads the "time of Jacob's trouble" as both the exile and all subsequent trials of God's people: "Every generation faces its own Jacob's trouble, and the promise applies universally: he will be saved out of it.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The promise of "David their king" is not a literal return of David from the dead but a messianic figure from David's line -- the Righteous Branch of 23:5. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads the Book of Consolation as deliberately structured to mirror the judgment oracles. Where chapters 2-25 tore down, chapters 30-31 build and plant -- fulfilling the constructive verbs of the original call (1:10). This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies the Book of Consolation as "prophetic imagination at its most daring." In the midst of national catastrophe, the prophet envisions a future that contradicts every present reality. This is not wishful thinking but faith in God's sovereign capacity to create newness.

hist.context: Chapters 30-31 are the "Book of Consolation" -- the heart of hope within the book of judgment. God commands Jeremiah to write these words in a scroll (v.2), emphasizing their permanent significance. After twenty-nine chapters of predominantly judgment oracles, the tone shifts dramatically. The promise is comprehensive: restoration of land, rebuilding of the city, regathering of the people, and a renewed relationship with God. Jacob's "time of trouble" (v.7) will end in deliverance.

sections.header: Verses 12-24 -- "I Will Restore You to Health"

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew uses אֲרֻכָה (arukah) meaning healing/restoration, not תְּעָלָה

refs.note: "Come, let us return to the LORD. He has torn us to pieces but he will heal us" -- Hosea's parallel healing promise.
"God's dwelling place is now among the people" -- the ultimate fulfillment of the covenant formula.

notes.note: The wound is first described as hopeless: incurable, no remedy, no advocate. The severity establishes that any healing must be divine in origin -- human resources have been exhausted.
"I will restore you to health" -- God himself becomes the physician. The balm in Gilead question (8:22) is finally answered: yes, there is a healer.
The rebuilt city, the thankful community, the ruler from among them, the covenant formula: "You will be my people, and I will be your God." Every element of covenant life is restored.

Calvin celebrates the reversal: "God first declares the wound incurable so that when he heals it, all glory belongs to grace alone. The impossibility of human remedy magnifies the power of divine mercy.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Their leader will be one of their own" -- the Hebrew qarib ("near one, from among them") contrasts with foreign rulers. The messianic figure will be a native leader, not an imposed outsider.
The covenant formula "you will be my people and I will be your God" appears over 20 times in the OT. Its placement here signals the renewal of the relationship that chapters 2-29 declared broken.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom traces the wound-healing arc across the book: diagnosis (8:22, "no balm?") -- despair (15:18, "deceptive brook") -- promise (30:17, "I will heal"). The Book of Consolation answers the questions raised in the judgment oracles. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies the healing promise as "the core verb of the Book of Consolation." The God who tears down (1:10) now heals. The same sovereignty that judged now restores. There is no change in God -- only a change in the moment. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The healing metaphor dominates vv.12-17: the wound is "incurable," with "no remedy," and "no one to plead your cause." But then the reversal: "I will restore you to health." The turning point is God's initiative, not Israel's merit. The rebuilt city (vv.18-22) will include a ruler "from among them" -- another messianic hint -- and the restored community will be "my people" and God will be "your God" (v.22), the covenant formula renewed.

rows.label: vv.1-3
rows.text: Command to Write: The Scroll of Hope

lit.note: The Book of Consolation opens with restoration promises that systematically answer the judgment oracles of chapters 1-29. The wound-healing arc (incurable to healed) is the key motif.

themes.note: Covenant and Mercy both peak at 9 -- the covenant formula renewed and the incurable wound healed. The dramatic shift from judgment to hope transforms the theological landscape.

hebtext.note: God promises to "restore the fortunes" (hashib et-shebut) of Israel and Judah (v.3). The phrase is ambiguous: shebut can mean "captivity" (a return from exile) or "fortune" (a restoration of prosperity). Both meanings operate simultaneously -- the return from physical exile coincides with spiritual renewal.

"I will restore you to health (te'ala) and heal your wounds" (v.17). The word te'ala means the fresh growth of flesh over a wound. After diagnosing an incurable wound (cf. 15:18, the "deceptive brook"), God now promises to heal what he declared unhealable. The healer was always present; the patient was unwilling. Now both willingness and healing converge.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Book of Consolation Begins

translations.text: The days are coming,’ declares the Lord, ‘when I will bring my people Israel and Judah back from captivity and restore them to the land I gave their ancestors to possess,’ says the Lord.”

For behold, days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will restore the fortunes of my people, Israel and Judah, says the LORD, and I will bring them back to the land that I gave to their fathers, and they shall take possession of it.”
“ ‘In that day,’ declares the Lord Almighty, ‘I will break the yoke off their necks and will tear off their bonds; no longer will foreigners enslave them.
“And it shall come to pass in that day, declares the LORD of hosts, that I will break his yoke from off your neck, and I will burst your bonds, and foreigners shall no more make a servant of him.

thread.text: "Comfort, comfort my people" -- Isaiah's parallel opening to the consolation oracles. Both prophets shift from judgment to hope at their rhetorical climax.
"In all things God works for the good" -- the NT echo of Jeremiah's assurance that suffering has a redemptive purpose.
"A time of distress such as has not happened" -- Daniel's "time of trouble" draws on Jeremiah's language.
"Come, let us return to the LORD. He has torn us to pieces but he will heal us" -- Hosea's parallel healing promise.
"God's dwelling place is now among the people" -- the ultimate fulfillment of the covenant formula.

coaching.tip: Chapters 30–33 form Jeremiah's 'Book of Consolation' — the most sustained passage of hope in the entire book. It's placed at the literary center, bracketed by judgment on both sides. The structure makes a theological statement: hope isn't an afterthought or appendix; it's the book's core, surrounded by the darkness that makes it necessary.

'I will restore you to health and heal your wounds' (v. 17). God speaks as physician to a patient everyone else has given up on — 'an outcast, Zion for whom no one cares.' The healing isn't earned or deserved; it comes precisely because no one else will help. Grace enters where human resources exit. The hopelessness of the patient is the occasion for the physician.

prayer_prompt: As God promises to restore the fortunes of His people and heal their wounds, consider asking God to show you the restoration He has already begun in your life — even if it is not yet complete.
